title: NATO Summit Crackdowns, Fidan Balances Moscow, and Hormuz Reopens on Turkiye's Doorstep
subhead: Ankara arrests dozens of anti-war activists ahead of the July NATO summit, FM Fidan tells Russia to stay out of the Black Sea while keeping diplomatic channels open, and the Strait of Hormuz reopening reshapes the energy calculus that directly affects Turkiye's economy.

sections.heading: Pre-Summit Crackdown: Dozens Arrested Ahead of NATO Ankara
sections.body:
Turkish authorities have detained at least 38 people across eight provinces in a series of police operations targeting anti-war and anti-NATO activists ahead of the July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara. According to the World Socialist Web Site, the operations began June 12 with raids in Eskisehir, Zonguldak, Balikesir, Karabuk, Kastamonu, Mardin, Mugla, and Van, where 14 students from the Revolutionary Youth Associations (DGD) were detained. Ten were subsequently jailed.

A second wave hit Istanbul on June 15, targeting members of the Independent Revolutionary Class Platform (BDSP), the Revolutionary Textile Workers Union (Dev Tekstil), and other left-wing organisations. Of the 23 people brought before a court on June 18, 21 were arrested — some without prosecutors even taking their statements.

Ankara has been declared a 'red zone' for the summit period. TRT reports that roughly 70,000 security personnel — including 55,000 police and gendarmerie — will be mobilised for approximately 6,000 summit participants. From July 6-12, all public events including exams, concerts, and festivals have been banned. The nationwide teaching examinations have been postponed to July 26.

The scope of the security operation — deploying a force larger than many NATO members' entire militaries, as Bloomberg noted — underscores how much political capital Erdogan is investing in hosting Trump and other leaders without disruption.
sections.whyItMatters: The mass detentions and the de facto state of emergency in Ankara signal that the Erdogan government will not tolerate visible dissent during the summit, regardless of the domestic political cost. The tactic of pre-emptive arrests — detaining people for activities they have not yet carried out — marks an escalation even by Turkiye's standards.

sections.heading: Fidan in Moscow: Warning Russia While Keeping the Door Open
sections.body:
Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan's visit to Moscow this week was the most consequential Turkey-Russia diplomatic encounter in months, coming just weeks before the NATO summit in Ankara. According to Reuters, Fidan held talks with Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov and President Vladimir Putin, and the agenda covered Ukraine, the Black Sea, and the South Caucasus.

Fidan told his Russian counterpart that Turkiye wants Moscow to avoid steps that threaten Black Sea security and Turkish interests, repeating Ankara's standing offer to host Russia-Ukraine peace talks. The WeeklyBlitz analysis noted that Fidan also discussed the evolving situation in Syria and Turkiye's Armenia normalisation efforts — both areas where Turkish and Russian interests have diverged sharply since Assad's collapse.

The visit highlighted the dual track of Ankara's Russia policy. On one hand, Fidan delivered a firm message on Black Sea militarisation and maritime safety. On the other, the fact that he sat with Putin at all — while Western leaders have largely frozen Moscow out — reinforces Turkiye's self-image as an indispensable interlocutor. Erdogan remains one of the few NATO leaders maintaining regular direct contact with Putin.
sections.whyItMatters: Fidan's Moscow trip was partly about sending a signal to Washington before the summit: Turkiye controls the diplomatic channel to Russia and intends to keep it open. But the Black Sea warning was a genuine red line — any escalation there directly threatens Turkish shipping through the Straits and the grain corridor arrangements Ankara brokered.

sections.heading: Hegseth's NATO Review: A Gathering Storm Before Ankara
sections.body:
US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth dropped a diplomatic grenade into NATO headquarters in Brussels on Thursday, announcing a six-month Pentagon review of US military forces in Europe and threatening to withhold US dues from NATO allies he considers free-riders. According to AP and Yahoo News, Hegseth called NATO a 'paper tiger' and a 'one-way street,' accused European allies of being 'shameful' for denying the US basing access for strikes on Iran, and railed against European gender equity and migration policies.

The review's outcome will supposedly depend on whether European allies 'take primary responsibility for the defense of Europe.' Hegseth said some countries 'will fail and others will pass with flying colors.' NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte could shed no light on what the review actually entails. Rutte noted that European allies and Canada spent $90 billion more on defense in 2025 than in 2024 — a 20% increase.

German Chancellor Friedrich Merz responded that European allies 'know we must do more and we are doing it.' The AP analysis pointed out that Hegseth's characterisation of European defense efforts was largely inaccurate, given the unprecedented spending increases already underway.

The timing is pointed: the review was announced less than three weeks before the Ankara summit on July 7-8. As AP noted, 'it does not augur well for a summit of NATO leaders in Turkey on July 7-8.'
sections.whyItMatters: Hegseth's review puts Erdogan in an awkward position as summit host. Turkiye has long called for more European defense spending — but it also benefits from the US military presence in the region and NATO cohesion. A fractious summit where the US publicly berates allies on Turkish soil is not the optics Erdogan wants.

sections.heading: Hormuz Reopens: Energy Relief and Strategic Questions for Ankara
sections.body:
The Strait of Hormuz is reopening to oil tanker traffic following the US-Iran ceasefire deal signed June 17, and the implications for Turkiye are direct. Reuters reports that oil shipments through the strait picked up on Friday, though questions remain about Iran's transit terms. CNBC reported that supertankers carrying nearly 80 million barrels of oil are queued in the Persian Gulf waiting to transit.

For Turkiye, the reopening matters on two fronts. First, the Iran war had driven up energy import costs — Turkiye depends on imported oil and gas, and any disruption at Hormuz feeds through to the lira and inflation. Second, the Istanbul Memorandum (signed in Islamabad with Pakistani and Qatari mediation) creates a new regional framework that Turkiye was not central to brokering — a contrast to its usual self-positioning as the region's indispensable mediator.

The New York Times reported that shipping companies hoping to move vessels through after the deal faced uncertainty on Friday, with Swiss-hosted follow-up talks between the US and Iran postponed amid the Israel-Hezbollah flare-up. Reuters separately noted that questions are growing about Iran's long-term transit terms for the strait.

Meanwhile, the Reuters analysis on Gulf exporters' quest to bypass Hormuz entirely — via pipelines and alternative routes — points to a longer-term shift that could reduce the strategic significance of the chokepoint Turkiye has no control over, while reinforcing the importance of alternative energy corridors through Turkish territory.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz reopening eases immediate energy price pressure on Turkiye's economy, but the fact that Turkiye was not a mediator in this deal — unlike the Ukraine grain deal or past Iran nuclear negotiations — is notable. Ankara's regional brokerage role faces competition from Doha and Islamabad.

sections.heading: Israel-Hezbollah Ceasefire: Fragile Calm on Turkiye's Southern Flank
sections.body:
Israel and Hezbollah agreed to a ceasefire on Friday, brokered by US and Qatari mediators, according to The Hindu and Reuters. The deal came after a day of intense violence: 47 people were killed in Israeli airstrikes in Lebanon, and four Israeli soldiers died — the highest combined casualty count since the US-Iran deal was signed.

The ceasefire is separate from the broader US-Iran framework and appears fragile. An Israeli minister declared that 'all of Lebanon must burn' after the soldiers' deaths, and the Swiss-hosted follow-up talks on the Iran deal were postponed due to the Lebanon fighting.

Turkiye has consistently called for de-escalation in Lebanon and has positioned itself as a supporter of Palestinian and Lebanese sovereignty. The rapid deterioration — from deal-signing to 47 dead to ceasefire in under 48 hours — illustrates the volatility on Turkiye's southern border and the limits of any agreement that does not address the underlying Israel-Hezbollah confrontation.
sections.whyItMatters: Every escalation in Lebanon increases refugee pressure on Turkiye (which already hosts over 3 million Syrians) and tests Ankara's diplomatic credibility. Turkiye's calls for restraint are consistent but have not translated into leverage over the parties.

sections.heading: Erdogan Opens Istanbul Airport Metro, Signals Infrastructure Commitment
sections.body:
President Erdogan officially opened the Halkali-Istanbul Airport Metro Line on Friday, using the ceremony to reaffirm his government's commitment to large-scale infrastructure investment. According to Ilha News, Erdogan said Turkiye 'will not allow Istanbul to be deprived of investment' and positioned the new line as part of a broader vision of Istanbul as a global summit and transport hub.

The metro opening comes amid the pre-summit beautification campaign that Bloomberg described as 'fresh paint and roses' in Ankara, and it reinforces the political message Erdogan wants to send visiting NATO leaders: that Turkiye is a country that delivers on infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: The metro line is both functional infrastructure and political theatre — Erdogan needs Istanbul looking modern and connected when world leaders arrive. The commitment to 'not allow Istanbul to be deprived of investment' is a direct response to opposition claims that the government has diverted resources away from Turkey's largest city.

bottomLine: A week before hosting the NATO summit, Turkiye is in full lockdown mode: mass arrests of anti-war activists, 70,000 security personnel mobilised, and public events banned across Ankara. FM Fidan walked a careful line in Moscow — warning Russia on Black Sea militarisation while preserving the diplomatic channel that gives Ankara leverage with Washington. Hegseth's surprise Pentagon review of US forces in Europe threatens to turn the Ankara summit into a confrontation rather than a show of unity. The Hormuz reopening eases energy cost pressure but exposes the fact that Turkiye was sidelined in the deal that mattered most this week — the Islamabad Memorandum was brokered by Pakistan and Qatar, not Ankara. The Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire is a relief, but its fragility means Turkiye's southern flank remains volatile.
sources: AP News, Reuters, Bloomberg, World Socialist Web Site, WeeklyBlitz, The Hindu, CNBC, New York Times, Yahoo News, RaillyNews, Ilha News
